summary: Cantrill argues that open source has entered a 'post-revolutionary' phase where GPLv2's strong copyleft provisions have become an anti-pattern that erects walls between projects rather than liberating code. He points to DTrace and ZFS's inability to be ported to Linux despite being open source for nearly a decade as evidence, and notes a surprising consensus emerging around moving to more collaboration-friendly licenses like MPLv2. The shift reflects open source's complete victory—young engineers no longer view proprietary software as an existential threat, so they prioritize collaboration over ideological purity.
key_points: GPLv2 creates barriers between open source projects rather than liberating proprietary ones
DTrace and ZFS remain unavailable on Linux despite being open source for years due to license incompatibility
Copyright assignment is a double-edged sword: it enables relicensing but concentrates power in a single actor
Major projects like VLC and Meteor have relicensed away from GPLv2 to enable broader collaboration
Open source has won so completely that younger engineers don't perceive proprietary software as a threat
money_quotes: I believe that the GPLv2 serves more to erect walls between open source projects than it does to liberate proprietary ones.
Open source is firmly in its post-revolutionary phase: it is so thoroughly mainstream that it is being entirely de-radicalized.
The triumph is so complete that the young software engineers of today simply don't see proprietary software as a threat.

Like an aspiring populace after a long period of conflict, the software engineers behind today's top open source projects are looking much more to a future of collaboration than to the past of internecine rivalries.
The open source revolution may be over, but that's because the war has been won – may we not squander its spoils!
key_insight: Open source licensing debates are shifting from ideological battles against proprietary software toward pragmatic choices that maximize collaboration between open source projects themselves.
